coredata.dc:title: Selective determination of Ag+ using Salecan derived nitrogen doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Junjian
Zuo, Gancheng
Qi, Xiaoliang
Wei, Wei
Pan, Xiaohao
Su, Ting
Zhang, Jianfa
Dong, Wei
coredata.dc:description: We report that nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) represent a viable fluorescent probe for the determination of Ag+. The N-CDs can be fairly easily prepared by a hydrothermal treatment method using Salecan and dicyandiamide as the precursors, display excitation/emission maxima of 350/438nm. The N-CDs have an average diameter of about 3.8nm. The N-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence (quantum yield of 13.2%) and could response to Ag+ selectively and sensitively. Other ions nearly had no interference. The probe has excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and a linear response range that extends from 0 to 40μM. The limit of detection is as low as 1.12×10−7 mol/L. The method was applied to the determination of Ag+ dissolved in water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salecan
Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Ag+ detection
Fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the Synthetic Strategy for N-CDs and the Principle of Ag+ Sensing.
(a) HR-TEM image and diameter distribution, (b) XRD spectrum of N-CDs.
XPS survey spectrum (a), C 1s (b), N 1s (c) XPS spectra of the as-prepared N-CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of N-CDs.

(a) optimal emission spectra of N-CDs. (b) Excitation dependent emission spectra of N-CDs. (c) Effect of ionic strengths on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. (d) Influence of pH values on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs.

(a) The fluorescence intensities of N-CDs in the presence of metal ions (1, Ag+; 2, Fe3+; 3, Pb2+; 4, Cd2+; 5, Cr3+; 6, Mg2+; 7, Cu2+; 8, Zn2+; 9, Ca2+; 10 Na+; 11K+; 12, Hg2+; 13, Ni2+; and 14, Co3+) (100μM), and following treatment of the mixture solution with 100.0μM Ag+. (b) Fluorescence titrations of the N-CDs (50μg·mL−1) with Ag+ from o to 40μM. (Inset: Quenching efficiency (i.e., decreases of emission intensity at 438nm) of the N-CDs with different concentrations of Ag+).
Determination results of N-CDs in real samples (n =3).

head.ce:title: Selective determination of Ag+ using Salecan derived nitrogen doped carbon dots as a fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Nanjing University of Science and Technology, Nanjing 210094, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report that nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) represent a viable fluorescent probe for the determination of Ag+. The N-CDs can be fairly easily prepared by a hydrothermal treatment method using Salecan and dicyandiamide as the precursors, display excitation/emission maxima of 350/438nm. The N-CDs have an average diameter of about 3.8nm. The N-CDs exhibited blue fluorescence (quantum yield of 13.2%) and could response to Ag+ selectively and sensitively. Other ions nearly had no interference. The probe has excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, and a linear response range that extends from 0 to 40μM. The limit of detection is as low as 1.12×10−7 mol/L. The method was applied to the determination of Ag+ dissolved in water.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent several years, as a late-model of fluorescent materials, quantum dots have aroused scientific attention [1–7]. CDs show distinguished features such as simple synthesis, low cost, low toxicity and good biocompatibility [8–11]. These particular characters endow CDs enormous potential applications, such as biomedicine, drug release and ions sensing [12–26]. Silver (Ag+) is an essential precious metal. Over the past decade, it has been wide used in the photographic and imaging industry [27,28]. Nevertheless, Ag+ as a heavy-metal ion could bring against effects to environment. Recently, selective determination of Ag+ signaling used CDs has been attracting extensive research interest.

Normally, important efforts have been exerted on the synthesis of CDs from a variety of carbon precursors by various methods, such as hydrothermal method, microwave method, thermal method, and so on [29–39]. In the midst of them, hydrothermal method is considered to be a simple and efficient way to obtain CDs.

Salecan was fabricated by Center for Molecular Metabolism, Nanjing University of Science & Technology. Dicyandiamide, AgNO3, CoCl3·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, CrCl3·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, ZnCl2, CaCl2, NaCl, KCl, NiCl2·6H2O, HgCl2 and FeCl3·6H2O were purchased from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification.

First, 0.5g of Salecan and 0.5g of dicyandiamide were dissolved in 30mL of water, and the solution was then transferred into a Teflon autoclave. After heating at 170°C for 8h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. After that, the solution was centrifugation at 4500rpm and 12,000rpm for 15min and collected by filtration (0.2μm cellulose membrane) respectively. The obtained N-CDs solution was subsequently dialyzed for 2days to remove all kinds of inorganic ions.

The detection of Fe3+ was conducted in Tris-HCl (10mM, pH=7.4) buffer solution at room temperature. In a typical assay, 50μg/mL of N-CDs dispersion was added into PBS, followed by the addition of Ag+ standard with various concentrations. The selectivity of Ag+ sensing was confirmed by adding other common metal ions stock solutions (Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Co3+) instead of Ag+ and determining in the same way.

The N-CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of the compound of Salecan and dicyandiamide. HRTEM characterizations were carried out to authentication our successfully obtaining N-CDs. HRTEM image of N-CDs shows well-dispersed in water solution. The particle size histogram shows the sizes to be around 2.5–5.5nm with an average size of 3.8nm and possess almost globosity. The inset in Fig. 2a showed the lattice spacing of 0.20nm. The typical XRD patterns of the N-CDs (Fig. 2b) display only one broader diffraction peak centered at 2θ=25.1°, which is also assigned to highly disordered carbon structure.

The chemical structure of N-CDs was further characterized by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum showed that C 1s, N 1s and O 1s signals appeared at 284.3, 399.2 and 531.0eV, respectively (Fig. 3a). Moreover, quantitative analysis of the N-CDs elements showed the following elemental composition: carbon (53.2%), nitrogen (10.6%) and oxygen (36.2%). The C 1s spectrum can be resolved as follows: CH at 283.34eV, CC/CN at 284.94eV, CO at 286.33eV and OCO at 287.60eV. The two fitted peaks at 529.82 and 531.26eV in O 1s spectrum were assigned to COH and CO groups, respectively. The N 1s peaks at 399.31 and 401.26eV indicated that nitrogen existed mostly in the form of NH and CN/NN, respectively. Simultaneously, the FTIR spectrum of N-CDs demonstrates the presence of the same groups as indicated by the XPS results. The peak at 1007cm−1 was ascribed to the CO bonds. The peak at 1417cm−1 can be ascribed to the CC group while the peak at 1663cm−1 was ascribed to the CO stretching vibration. In addition, the broad band at 3277cm−1 corresponded to the stretching modes of NH and OH. All results of XPS and FTIR spectra clearly substantiated that N atoms had perfectly doped in N-CDs.

In the fluorescence spectrum, the optimal excitation and emission wavelength are at 350 and 438nm. Excitation-dependent Fluorescence intensity behavior is observed, which is common in fluorescent carbon materials. The emission wavelength shifts from 430 to 510nm when the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 460nm. The fluorescence intensity strengthened when the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 350nm. The fluorescence intensity receded when the excitation wavelength increases from 360 to 460nm. (Fig. 4b). The QY of N-CDs is determined to be 13.2% by selecting the quinine sulfate as standard. The stability of both N-CDs under various conditions has also been studied. As seen in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs almost remains constant by diverse the NaCl concentration, indicating the good stability of N-CDs even in high ionic strength environment. The fluorescent properties of N-CDs are pH dependent (Fig. 4d). The N-CDs displayed stable fluorescence in the pH range 2–10, indicating that the N-CDs can be employed in both acidic and weak alkaline environments. Under strong alkali conditions, the fluorescence intensity is nearly fully quenched. The effect of the pH values can be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to deprotonation.

The fluorescence intensity decreasing effect of various ions on N-CDs was studied. Different ions including Ag+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+, Ni2+ and Co3+ of each at a concentration of 100μM were reacted with N-CDs solution (50μg/mL). As shown in Fig. 5a, it could be seen that a much lower fluorescence was observed for N-CDs upon addition of Ag+ while other ions showed either no change in the fluorescence intensity relative to the free N-CDs. Among these ions, Ag+ showed the strongest fluorescence intensity decreasing effect on N-CDs. Furthermore, no obvious interference was found even with high concentrations (up to 100μM) of other metal ions. Titration experiment show that the fluorescence intensity decreased stepwise with the increase of the concentration of Ag+. Fig. 5b presents the intensity versus the concentration of Ag+. There exists a fine linear correlation (R2 =0.9932) between the intensity and the concentration of Ag+ in the range of 0 to 40μM. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 1.12×10−7 mol/L based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3Sd/s).

In order to examine the applicability of the proposed method in practical sample analysis, the N-CDs were applied in the measure of Ag+ in water samples. All water samples were spiked with standard Ag+ solutions and then analyzed with N-CDs. Table 1 show that the recovery study of Ag+ is measured by the N-CDs. The results show that the N-CDs have quantitative measure of Ag+ in water.

In summary, we reported a one-step synthesis of N-CDs by the hydrothermal treatment of Salecan and dicyandiamide. Most importantly, N-CDs can be used as a simple and effective fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+, and further extended to measure environmental water samples. The N-CDs are employed for assaying Ag+, and a linear relationship between the decreased fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Ag+ is established in the range from 40μM. The detection limit is as low as 1.12×10−7 mol/L, suggesting a promising assay for Ag+. We believe that the proposed N-CDs sensors will be more useful upon further development.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC: 51573078) are gratefully acknowledged.
